obstruction_types.hk_legal_basis: Hong Kong follows the English common law doctrine of consideration. A contract is generally not enforceable without consideration (or a deed). However, the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance (Cap. 623) and the doctrine of promissory estoppel provide limited exceptions. There is no general statutory abolition of the consideration requirement.
key_statutes: Cap. 623 Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Ordinance
key_cases: Rann v Hughes (1778) 7 T.R. 350 n. (applied in HK)

obstruction_types.hk_legal_basis: Specific performance is an equitable remedy available in Hong Kong where damages are not an adequate remedy. It is discretionary and generally not granted for contracts requiring personal services or continuous supervision. The court follows English equitable principles. Under the CISG (Cap. 641), specific performance is available but subject to the forum court's discretion under Article 28.
key_statutes: Cap. 641 Sale of Goods (United Nations Convention) Ordinance
key_cases: Hummingbird Music Ltd v Accounci [2010] 1 HKLRD 587 (discussed specific performance principles)

obstruction_types.hk_legal_basis: Punitive (exemplary) damages are available in Hong Kong in limited circumstances, primarily for oppressive, arbitrary or unconstitutional action by government servants, and for calculated wrongdoing in tort. The courts follow the House of Lords' approach in Rookes v Barnard [1964] AC 1129. There is no general prohibition, but the scope is narrower than in some US jurisdictions.
key_cases: Rookes v Barnard [1964] AC 1129 (applied in HK)
Cheng v Tse Wai Chun (2000) 3 HKCFAR 339

obstruction_types.hk_legal_basis: Discovery in Hong Kong civil procedure is governed by Order 24 of the High Court Rules (Cap. 4A). Parties must disclose all documents relevant to matters in question, subject to privilege. The scope is broad but not as extensive as US-style discovery. There is no general pre-action disclosure regime, but the court may order specific discovery. The court is moving toward more proportionate disclosure.
key_cases: Three Rivers District Council v Bank of England (No.6) [2005] 1 AC 610 (applied in HK)

obstruction_types.hk_legal_basis: Legal professional privilege (LPP) in Hong Kong includes legal advice privilege and litigation privilege. It is constitutionally protected under Article 35 of the Basic Law (right to confidential legal advice). LPP can be waived by the client. In FACV 8/2023, the CFA held that LPP is a fundamental right but subject to exceptions and waiver. The Law Society's disciplinary powers may justify limited abrogation by necessary implication.
key_cases: Shum Kwok Sher v HKSAR (2002) 5 HKCFAR 381

obstruction_types.hk_legal_basis: Hong Kong does NOT recognize 'employment at will'. All employment contracts are subject to the Employment Ordinance (Cap. 57), which provides statutory protections including: minimum notice periods, statutory holidays, sick leave, maternity/paternity leave, severance pay, and protection against unreasonable dismissal (after 24 months continuous service). Dismissal must be for a valid reason under s.32K. This is a significant difference from US employment law.
key_statutes: Cap. 57 Employment Ordinance, ss.6-7 (notice), s.32K (valid reasons)

obstruction_types.hk_legal_basis: Cross-border data transfer from Hong Kong is regulated by section 33 of the PDPO (Cap. 486), but this section has NOT yet been brought into force. Pending activation, data users should comply with Data Protection Principles 1 and 3. The Privacy Commissioner has issued guidance recommending that data users obtain consent and ensure adequate protection in the destination jurisdiction. Unlike the EU GDPR, there is no active adequacy decision regime.

Hong Kong does not have a general 'blocking statute' comparable to the EU Blocking Statute. However, the National Security Law (HKNSL) and the Safeguarding National Security Ordinance (2024) contain provisions that may restrict compliance with foreign sanctions or orders that endanger national security. The Trade Diversion Ordinance and other trade control laws may have limited blocking effects. There is no comprehensive statute preventing disclosure for foreign proceedings.

Hong Kong implements UN sanctions through the United Nations Sanctions Ordinance (Cap. 537) and various subsidiary regulations. The Hong Kong Monetary Authority and Customs and Excise Department enforce financial sanctions. Hong Kong does not automatically implement unilateral sanctions imposed by other jurisdictions (e.g., US, EU). Financial institutions must comply with AML/CTF requirements under Cap. 615.
key_statutes: Cap. 615 Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance

obstruction_types.hk_legal_basis: Hong Kong does not have a specific statutory safe harbor for online platform liability comparable to Section 230 of the US Communications Decency Act or the EU E-Commerce Directive. Platform liability is generally determined by common law principles of secondary liability (authorisation, joint tortfeasance) and the specific statutory framework for each type of content (e.g., copyright infringement under Cap. 528, defamation). The Electronic Transactions Ordinance (Cap. 553) provides limited liability for intermediaries in relation to electronic records.

Hong Kong recognizes joint and several liability in tort. The Law Reform Commission has considered proportionate liability reforms (particularly for professional negligence) but these have not been enacted as general law. The Limited Liability Partnerships Ordinance (Cap. 637) allows professional firms to limit liability to the partnership assets. Class actions are not generally available in Hong Kong, though the LRC has recommended their introduction.
key_cases: Law Reform Commission Report on Class Actions

obstruction_types.hk_legal_basis: Hong Kong does NOT have a formal plea bargaining system comparable to the US. The Secretary for Justice has a general discretion under s.15 of the Criminal Procedure Ordinance (Cap. 221) to enter a nolle prosequi. In practice, there is informal charge bargaining and sentence indication procedures, but no binding plea agreements. The Court of Appeal in Secretary for Justice v Wong Chak Kei [2019] HKCA 1123 discussed the limited scope of sentence indications. The prosecution retains the right to appeal against sentence on the ground of manifest inadequacy.
key_cases: Secretary for Justice v Wong Chak Kei [2019] HKCA 1123

notes: This analysis identifies key differences between Hong Kong and other common law jurisdictions (particularly the US and UK) that may create legal obstructions in cross-border litigation or compliance.
